common_name: Flordaglo Nectarine (University of Florida Very Low-Chill Yellow Nectarine for Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; Flordaglo requires only 150–200 chilling hours — among the absolute lowest chill requirements of any nectarine variety, making it one of the only nectarines reliably producible in zone 9b-10 where mild winters rarely accumulate the 500–700 hours needed by standard nectarines. In zone 9 on the Gulf Coast, Flordaglo ripens in late May to early June — very early for a nectarine. The tree is self-fertile and does not require a cross-pollinator, though yield may improve with nearby compatible stone fruit. As with all nectarines in humid climates, bacterial spot and brown rot can be significant; preventive copper and fungicide sprays during bloom and leaf-out help maintain plant health and fruit quality in the Gulf South's humid conditions.

directions: Plant bare-root tree in late winter in full sun, well-drained soil. Thin fruit to 6–8 inches apart. Harvest when fully yellow-orange and gives slightly to pressure. Flordaglo Nectarine was released by the University of Florida IFAS as part of the same low-chill stone fruit breeding program that produced Flordaking, Flordaprince, and other Gulf South-adapted peach and nectarine varieties. Nectarines (smooth-skinned peaches, genetically a single-gene variant of the standard fuzzy peach) are typically even more demanding in their chill hour requirements than their peach counterparts, making the development of a reliable very low-chill nectarine a significant achievement. With approximately 150–200 chilling hours required, Flordaglo can be grown in most of Florida and the Gulf Coast — conditions under which virtually all standard nectarine varieties fail to bloom and set properly. The fruit is medium-sized, with a yellow-red skin and yellow freestone flesh — the smooth nectarine skin distinguishing it visually from the closely related fuzzy peaches. The flavor is typically sweeter and slightly more aromatic than many standard low-chill peaches, with the characteristic nectarine quality that growers in cool climates take for granted but that gardeners in zone 9 rarely get to experience from home-grown fruit. For zone 9 home orchardists who want to grow nectarines — generally considered nearly impossible in the Gulf South — Flordaglo is one of the very few varieties with a realistic chance of success.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Flordaglo nectarine low-chill variety development
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Nectarine and Peach Variety Recommendations for the Gulf South
Dave Wilson Nursery — Flordaglo nectarine low-chill commercial variety notes

common_name: Au Amber Plum (Auburn University Amber-Skinned Japanese-American Hybrid Plum for Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; Au Amber requires a cross-pollinator for good fruit set — plant with Au Rosa, Methley, or another Japanese or Japanese-American plum within 50 feet. Requires approximately 400–500 chilling hours, placing it reliably in the productive range for most zone 8 and the cooler parts of zone 9 in north Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama. In the warmest zone 9b locations, chill accumulation may be marginal in mild winters; zone 8 locations typically produce reliably. Ripens June in zone 9. Plant in full sun in very well-drained soil — like all Prunus species, Au Amber is highly susceptible to root rot in poorly drained conditions. The Auburn University Au-series plums were bred specifically for the disease pressure, humidity, and modest chill of the Gulf South, giving them practical advantages over Japanese plums bred for Pacific Coast or California conditions.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; requires cross-pollinator within 50 feet. Thin fruit clusters to 3–4 inches. Harvest when amber-yellow skin has fully developed and flesh gives slightly. Au Amber Plum is one of the Auburn University Agricultural Experiment Station's series of Japanese-American plum hybrids bearing the 'Au' prefix — a family of Gulf South-adapted plum varieties developed by crossing Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) with American plum (Prunus americana) to combine quality fruit with regional disease and humidity tolerance. The 'Amber' designation refers to the fruit's distinctive amber-yellow to golden skin, which sets it apart from the red and rose-colored Au-series varieties (Au Rosa, Au Rubrum) within the same breeding program and from the predominantly red-skinned Japanese plum varieties available in American nurseries. The amber-yellow coloring of the ripe fruit is unusual among commonly available plums — most market plums are red, purple, or dark-skinned — making Au Amber visually distinctive both in the garden and at market or farmers markets where consumers are accustomed to seeing darker plum colors. The flesh is yellow-amber, juicy, and sweet with mild plum flavor appropriate for fresh eating, jams, and jellies. The relatively high chilling requirement within the Au-series makes Au Amber better suited to zone 8 and the cooler parts of zone 9 than the lower-chill Au Rosa, providing a range of options across the Gulf South's varied climate zones. Auburn University developed the Au series specifically based on performance trials in Alabama, and the varieties' continued presence in extension service recommendations decades after their introduction reflects their practical suitability to the region.
sources: Auburn University Agricultural Experiment Station — Au-series plum variety documentation
Alabama Cooperative Extension System — Stone Fruit Variety Recommendations for Alabama
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Plum Variety Selection for the Gulf South and Midsouth

common_name: Friar Plum (Dark-Skinned California Japanese Plum with Mild Sweet Amber Flesh)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; Friar requires a cross-pollinator for good fruit set — plant with Santa Rosa, Burgundy, Methley, or another compatible Japanese plum within 50 feet. Requires approximately 400–500 chilling hours, placing it on the upper end of what zone 9 reliably provides in most years; it performs most consistently in zone 8 and cooler zone 9 locations with 500+ chill hours most winters. In the Gulf South, Friar ripens July — one of the latest Japanese plum varieties to mature, following the earlier summer plums by several weeks and extending the fresh plum season. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage. Friar Plum is widely available in California-adapted nursery stock and from mail-order nurseries serving the southeastern US.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; requires cross-pollinator. Thin fruit to 4–5 inches. Harvest when skin is fully dark purple-black and fruit gives slightly under firm pressure. Friar Plum is a California-developed Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released by the University of California Cooperative Extension in the 1960s as part of a systematic program of plum variety evaluation and selection for California commercial production — a program that produced several varieties (Santa Rosa, Friar, Laroda, Casselman) that became mainstays of California plum production and eventually found their way into home garden cultivation throughout the United States. Friar is distinguished by its very dark, near-black skin — the dark purple-black coloring that covers the entire fruit surface at maturity — contrasting with the amber-yellow flesh beneath, which creates a dramatic visual contrast when the fruit is cut. The skin color of Friar is among the darkest of any common Japanese plum variety. The flesh is firm, sweet, and mildly flavored with moderate acidity — less acid than Santa Rosa — creating a mild, sweet eating character that appeals to consumers who prefer less tangy plum flavor. The fruit is large (often 2–2.5 inches), globe-shaped, and produced in good quantity on a moderately vigorous tree. Friar's late ripening (July in California, slightly earlier in the humid South due to the longer season) helps extend the Japanese plum harvest season beyond the earlier-ripening varieties, providing fresh market plums during a period when earlier types have finished. In zone 9, Friar benefits from the long growing season but requires adequate chill to set reliably.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Friar plum California variety performance data
Dave Wilson Nursery — Friar plum commercial variety notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Plum Variety Selection for Texas

common_name: Madame Jeanette Pepper (Surinamese Lantern Pepper, Intensely Hot Caribbean Capsicum chinense)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; Madame Jeanette, like all Capsicum chinense, requires very warm germination temperatures (80–95°F) on a heat mat and is slow to germinate compared to C. annuum — allow 14–21 days. In zone 9, transplant March–April into full sun. Capsicum chinense thrives in Gulf Coast heat and may overwinter as a perennial in zone 9b if protected from frost, sometimes producing for 2–3 seasons and yielding heavier crops in subsequent years. Harvest when fruits have fully transitioned from green to their ripe yellow color — do not harvest green. The heat level (150,000–300,000 SHU) makes Madame Jeanette one of the hotter commonly grown Capsicum chinense varieties; handle with gloves and avoid touching eyes or face.

directions: Start seeds on heat mat at 85–90°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Harvest when fully yellow. Madame Jeanette is a Surinamese Capsicum chinense variety that is one of the most important chiles in Dutch-Caribbean cuisine — particularly in the culinary traditions of the Surinamese and Antillean communities that immigrated to the Netherlands beginning in the mid-20th century. The pepper is named after a legendary entertainer from Paramaribo, Suriname's capital, and carries strong cultural significance in Surinamese cooking, where it is used to season traditional dishes such as pom (tayer root casserole) and various rice preparations. Morphologically, Madame Jeanette produces elongated, lantern-shaped to irregular pendant fruits — 2–3 inches long, tapering to a point — that ripen from green to yellow (occasionally to orange or red in some strains). The shape is somewhat different from the round, wrinkled habanero and the bell-shaped Scotch bonnet, falling between those types and the elongated fatalii in form. The heat level is substantial (estimated 150,000–300,000 Scoville units) with the characteristic fruity, floral habanero-type aroma of C. chinense — sweet, tropical, and intensely fragrant. The flavor that accompanies the heat is distinctly fruity and aromatic with apricot-like qualities that distinguish C. chinense from the more neutral heat of cayenne. In zone 9, Madame Jeanette performs exceptionally well — the heat and humidity that challenge cool-season crops are ideal conditions for this tropical species, which can produce abundant fruit through summer and fall.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Madame Jeanette pepper Surinamese variety documentation
Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University — Capsicum chinense heat levels and Caribbean varieties
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Madame Jeanette pepper variety notes

common_name: Lipstick Pepper (AAS-Winning Sweet Pimento-Type Pepper, Compact and Prolific)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Lipstick is a compact sweet pepper producing small to medium pimento-shaped fruits (about 4 inches long, heart-shaped with a pointed tip). In zone 9, transplant March–April. Lipstick produces prolifically on compact plants (18–24 inches) suitable for containers and small gardens. Harvest green or allow to fully ripen to deep red for sweetest flavor — red-ripe Lipstick has exceptional sweetness and thick walls ideal for roasting, fresh eating, and stuffing. The compact plant habit means Lipstick can be grown in 5-gallon containers on patios, making it useful for zone 9 gardeners without significant ground space. Unlike the large, blocky bell pepper types that often struggle to set fruit in zone 9's summer heat, the pimento type peppers like Lipstick are generally more heat-tolerant and continue producing through the Gulf Coast summer.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest green or red; red is sweeter. Lipstick Pepper is an All-America Selections (AAS) winner in the sweet pepper category, recognized for its combination of compact plant size, prolific productivity, and exceptional fruit quality in the pimento pepper class. The variety belongs to the pimento type — a group of sweet peppers distinct from bell peppers in fruit shape (heart-shaped, conical, or tapered rather than blocky), flesh thickness (very thick walls with small seed cavity), and flavor (sweeter, with less of the grassy quality of green bell peppers). Pimento peppers have a long history in American cooking: the red pimento paste used to stuff olives and fill pimento cheese sandwiches is made from this type, and the pimento's thick, sweet walls make it the preferred pepper for roasting, where the flesh caramelizes to an exceptional sweetness. Lipstick's AAS recognition was awarded specifically for its performance across diverse North American climates — including hot-summer regions where standard bell peppers underperform — making it a practical choice for gardeners in zone 9 who find that large bell peppers drop their blossoms or produce poorly during the Gulf South's summer heat peak. The fruit is medium-sized (3–4 inches long, 2.5 inches wide), heart-shaped to conical with a small pointed tip, and ripens from green to a deep, brilliant red. The skin is thinner than a bell pepper and the flesh is proportionally thicker, with a small, easily removed seed cavity. The flavor at red-ripe stage is very sweet, almost candy-like, with none of the bitterness that some tasters detect in green bell peppers.
sources: All-America Selections — Lipstick pepper AAS recognition and performance trials
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Lipstick pimento pepper variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Sweet Pepper Variety Selection for Home Gardens
